perturbation.original_text: 1. The word "Buyer”, as used herein shall mean and include the undersigned individually, and any partnership, limited liability company, corporation, or any other business entity identified below.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. The word "Buyer”, as used herein shall only mean and include the undersigned individually, and specifically exclude any partnership, limited liability company, corporation, or any other business entity identified below.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the definition of 'Buyer' to exclude business entities contradicts common legal interpretations and Pennsylvania law regarding business ownership and contractual obligations. This creates uncertainty regarding who is bound by the agreement if the individual acts on behalf of a business.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania Uniform Partnership Act, 15 Pa. C.S. § 8103
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the Pennsylvania Uniform Partnership Act, specifically regarding the definition of a partnership as an entity distinct from its partners. By excluding partnerships from the definition of 'Buyer,' the contract potentially fails to bind the partnership itself, which is contrary to the legal understanding of partnerships.

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly excludes business entities from the definition of 'Buyer', which contradicts the general legal understanding that a business entity can be a party to a contract. The Law Explanation asserts this contradicts the Pennsylvania Uniform Partnership Act, potentially failing to bind the partnership itself. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is repealed, the Perturbation and Law Explanations outline how the exclusion of business entities undermines established legal principles regarding contractual obligations of partnerships, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2. The Information is of a proprietary and confidential nature, the disclosure of which to any other party could result in damages to the Seller and/or Business. (a) The Information furnished by Broker or Seller has not been publicly disclosed, has not been made available to Buyer by any party or source other than Broker or Seller and is being furnished only upon the terms and conditions contained in this Agreement; (b) Buyer will not disclose the Information, in whole or in part, to any party other than persons within Buyer's organization, including independent advisers/consultants, who have a need to know such Information for purposes of evaluating or structuring the possible purchase of the Business.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. The Information is considered generally available, and Buyer assumes all responsibility for its secure handling. (a) The Information furnished by Broker or Seller may have been previously disclosed publicly. (b) Buyer has the right to disclose the Information, in whole or in part, to any party they deem appropriate, without restriction.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the nature of the information from 'proprietary and confidential' to 'generally available' and removing restrictions on disclosure directly contradicts the core purpose of a non-disclosure agreement. This directly impacts the enforceability of confidentiality clauses and related legal protections.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania Uniform Trade Secrets Act, 12 Pa.C.S. § 5302
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification directly contradicts the Pennsylvania Uniform Trade Secrets Act by removing the confidential status of the information. This weakens the seller's ability to protect its trade secrets under Pennsylvania law, as the information is now considered publicly available.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5302 (2022) - Definitions :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5302 - Definitions Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5302 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5302. Definitions. The following words and phrases when used in this chapter shall have the meanings given to them in this section unless the context clearly indicates otherwise: "Improper means." Includes, but is not limited to, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation." Includes: (1) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. "Person." A natural person, corporation,
perturbation.justification: The modified text fundamentally contradicts the original agreement by changing the nature of the information from 'proprietary and confidential' to 'generally available' and removing restrictions on its disclosure. This directly undermines the protection of trade secrets as defined in Pennsylvania law. The original agreement aimed to maintain secrecy, while the modified text permits unrestricted disclosure. The Perturbation Explanation clearly states that this modification contradicts the core purpose of a non-disclosure agreement and impacts the enforceability of confidentiality clauses. The Law Explanation highlights that this weakens the seller's ability to protect its trade secrets under Pennsylvania law. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'misappropriation' to include the disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent. The changed text, by allowing unrestricted disclosure, directly conflicts with the duty to maintain secrecy implied in the original agreement and potentially leads to misappropriation as defined by 12 PA Cons Stat § 5302.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Buyer will indemnify and hold harmless the Broker and Seller from any and all claims or actions arising from Buyer's acts or failures to act in pursuing the possible purchase of the Business, including, without limitation, reasonable attorney's fees and other expenses incurred by Broker and seller.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Buyer is only required to indemnify the Broker and Seller for direct damages proven to be the result of gross negligence or willful misconduct on the part of the Buyer, and specifically excluding attorney's fees.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the indemnification clause to require 'gross negligence or willful misconduct' and excluding attorney's fees significantly narrows the scope of protection for the Broker and Seller. This increases their potential exposure to liability and contradicts typical indemnification agreements which often cover a broader range of actions.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification makes the indemnification clause less encompassing, deviating from the protections generally available to indemnitees under Pennsylvania law which permit indemnification for a wider range of actions not just gross negligence or willful misconduct.
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly narrows the scope of indemnification, requiring 'gross negligence or willful misconduct' and excluding attorney's fees. This deviation from typical indemnification agreements, which usually cover a broader range of actions, creates a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation supports that the change makes the indemnification clause less encompassing, deviating from the protections generally available to indemnitees under Pennsylvania law, permitting indemnification for a wider range of actions not just gross negligence or willful misconduct.

perturbation.original_text: 11. Any controversy between the parties arising out of the terms, obligations, covenants or conditions of this Agreement, shall be arbitrated in Bucks County, Pennsylvania on written request of one (1) party served on the other. Such arbitration shall be under the rules of the American Arbitration Association. The arbitrator shall have no authority to change any provisions of this agreement; the arbitrator’s sole authority shall be to interpret or apply the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. Any controversy between the parties arising out of the terms, obligations, covenants or conditions of this Agreement, shall be resolved through non-binding mediation, with each party bearing their own costs, in a location of the defendant's choosing.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the dispute resolution method from binding arbitration to non-binding mediation significantly alters the enforceability of the agreement. The addition of 'location of the defendant's choosing' adds potential for unfairness. This creates an environment where disputes are more difficult to resolve efficiently and legally.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania Uniform Arbitration Act, 42 Pa. C.S. § 7303
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification prevents the binding arbitration stipulated under the Pennsylvania Uniform Arbitration Act by making mediation non-binding. This weakens enforcement mechanisms provided under state law, particularly the ability to confirm and enforce arbitration awards through court proceedings.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 42 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 7303 (2022) - Validity of agreement to arbitrate :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 42 - JUDICIARY AND JUDICIAL PROCEDURE Chapter 73 - Arbitration Section 7303 - Validity of agreement to arbitrate Universal Citation: 42 PA Cons Stat § 7303 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 7303. Validity of agreement to arbitrate. A written agreement to subject any existing controversy to arbitration or a provision in a written agreement to submit to arbitration any controversy thereafter arising between the parties is valid, enforceable and irrevocable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity relating to the validity, enforceability or revocation of any contract. Cross References. Section 7303 is referred to in sections 7304, 7318 of this title. Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. perturbation.justification: The modification changes dispute resolution from binding arbitration to non-binding mediation, directly contradicting the Pennsylvania Uniform Arbitration Act. Scraped Law Snippet 2, specifically 42 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 7303, states that an agreement to arbitrate is 'valid, enforceable and irrevocable'. By making mediation non-binding, the modified contract undermines the enforceability guaranteed by this statute. The original agreement provided a legally binding mechanism for dispute resolution, which the changed text nullifies, thus establishing a legal contradiction.
